data.title: Sofa so bad: China’s home-furnishing sector hits a do-or-die stretch
data.description:
China’s more than 100,000 home furnishing companies - most of which are micro businesses - have entered a tumultuous phase as a property slump and persistent deflationary pressure prevent them from making profits.
With no property resurgence in sight, the industry, which employs millions of labourers across the country, was likely to see thousands of players expelled from the highly competitive market this year, according to company officials and analysts.
“Involution is the name of the game in the home decor market,” said Yan Jun, a senior executive with Shanghai-based Jinghan Stainless Products, which designs and makes steel windows for household use. “Clients want to save furnishing costs and keep trying to bargain down prices while more competitors, who are desperate to get business, are willing to offer huge discounts.”
In mainland China’s business lexicon, involution refers to fierce competition that drives down prices and undermines sustainable growth. In some cases, manufacturers sell products below cost just to keep rivals at bay.
Home furnishing and residential property are closely intertwined in China, as a buoyant construction industry and sizzling housing sales once ushered in surging demand for home decor.
According to Hunan Zonghengjia Brand Consulting, the mainland’s home decor sector generated revenue of 2.68 trillion yuan (US$387.9 billion) in 2025, up 2.8 per cent year on year.
The National Bureau of Statistics said last year that China’s homebuilding and decor sector provided about 50 million jobs, adding that roughly a quarter of the workforce was engaged in furnishing-related businesses.
A senior manager at Shanghai Jutong Decoration, one of the city’s largest furnishing firms, said falling home prices had dealt a devastating blow to business, as flat owners sharply cut budgets for home improvement over the past three years.
In Shanghai, homeowners typically used to spend about 20 per cent of a property’s value on decoration, according to You Liangzhou, owner of property agency Baonuo. “Now they are very cautious about furnishing spending and most cut budgets to just 10 per cent, or even less, of the home’s value.”

Average lived-in home prices in Shanghai, the mainland’s commercial and financial hub, had slumped more than 28 per cent from their 2023 peak to 42,749 yuan per square metre at the end of last year, according to the Anjuke Real Estate Research Institute.
Zonghengjia Brand Consulting said in a February report that the country’s largest 1,000 home-furnishing companies, which enjoyed greater bargaining power with suppliers and clients, could improve performance by using digital technologies to cut operating costs.
But a growing number of small decoration firms faced a do-or-die moment this year amid intensifying competition, it added.
The property industry remained a drag on China’s economic growth because of its significant impact on employment and consumer demand, according to industry officials and analysts.
Amid sharp declines in property investment, housing sales and home prices last year, a further downturn in the sector could put more developers, workers, homebuyers and banks at risk.
In 2025, new properties worth 8.4 trillion yuan, comprising residential and commercial units, were sold across mainland China, down 13 per cent from a year earlier, the NBS said in January.
The property sector and related industries, such as home appliances and construction materials, account for about a quarter of the mainland’s economic output.
Chinese consumers had kept their purse strings tight since 2023 amid worries about US-China trade tensions and a prolonged property slump that had wiped out a major source of household wealth, according to a study released by global consultancy Bain & Company and market research firm Kantar Worldpanel in December.
China’s housing market, after three decades of breakneck growth, began retreating in late 2020 when Beijing introduced measures to rein in excessive leverage among developers and prevent a financial shock to the economy.
Since October 2021, dozens of developers, including China Evergrande Group and Country Garden Holdings, have defaulted on loans and bonds, while many buyers have held back from purchasing both new and second-hand homes.

data.title: Workers ready to sacrifice Lunar New Year break as scaffold net rules bite
data.description:
Hong Kong plumber Anson Hui Po-lung is ready to work through his Lunar New Year break to make ends meet, after struggling since the citywide removal of scaffolding nets began in early December, in response to the deadly blaze at Tai Po’s Wang Fuk Court.
Twenty public housing projects and 230 private buildings undergoing major maintenance had to suspend facade work after the removal of the nets, threatening the livelihoods of about 700 workers for more than two months.
As of mid-February, only 19 private buildings and six public housing projects have reinstalled nets under a new mechanism requiring stringent fire safety checks.
Before the Lunar New Year holiday began on Tuesday, Hui said he had yet to hear from his employer about resuming facade pipe work, which accounts for 70 per cent of his HK$40,000 (US$5,118) monthly income.
Currently earning about HK$10,000 a month from small‑scale interior pipe repairs, the 51-year-old said he could not afford new clothes or make travel plans for his children.
He still had to pay alimony to his ex-wife, cover the living expenses of his elderly mother and settle the rent, he added.
“If there is any emergency repair during the Lunar New Year holiday, I will still work as I can earn more money … Previously, I took a one-week break,” he said.
Labour unions and contractors have raised concerns about a potential decline in large-scale maintenance work in the coming year, citing waning confidence among property owners after the fire at Wang Fuk Court, a subsidised estate that had been under repair, killed 168 people and prompted a revamp of the maintenance regulatory regime.
The proposal will be tabled before the Legislative Council in the latter half of the year, but industry stakeholders have urged the government to expedite the process, warning that delays would threaten workers’ livelihoods and dampen business.

Ahead of the new regime, authorities have required contractors to procure mesh with certified fire-retardant reports and have net samples tested at designated laboratories.
The material can only be installed once all samples pass the tests, which must be repeated every 12 months.
As of Monday, the Construction Industry Council (CIC), which has arranged a one-off procurement of mesh for contractors, had received applications from 260 construction sites, with about 50,000 nets ready for collection before the holiday began.
The Buildings Department said it had received notices from 19 of the 230 affected private buildings regarding mesh installation works so far, adding that one block had been selected for random checks.
The Housing Bureau said that among the 20 public housing projects affected by the mesh removal, six had reinstalled nets as of last Friday. Five projects were completed without reinstalling the mesh, while work on the remaining nine estates would be “carried out progressively”.
The Architectural Services Department said it would reinstall nets on one of the two affected government buildings next month, while installation for the other project was scheduled for late 2026.
To help affected workers, the CIC has provided 5,000 spots under a training scheme. Those who obtain the relevant qualifications can receive an allowance of up to HK$19,000.
Hui has applied for a metal scaffolding course, but classes will not begin until May.
The Hong Kong Construction Industry Employees General Union said it had received around 700 reports of unemployment or underemployment stemming from the suspension of building maintenance projects.




While the labour union has referred applicants to training courses, chairman Chau Sze-kit said some workers were relying on their savings or had returned to mainland China for family reunions.
“Some contractors still have to buy nets on their own or are still negotiating with owners on the financial responsibility. We expect that work can only be fully resumed after the Lunar New Year holiday,” Chau said.
Lawmaker Lam Chun-sing, chairman of the Federation of Hong Kong and Kowloon Labour Unions, said his group had handled more than 20 similar cases, mainly involving plumbers and electrical and mechanical workers.
He said some affected workers had taken on odd jobs or temporary security work.
While buildings with ongoing repair projects would gradually resume operations, Lam noted that some estates had put maintenance on hold.
For instance, owners at Elegance Garden in Tuen Mun terminated their renovation contract with Prestige Construction and Engineering Company, the same contractor responsible for work at Wang Fuk Court.
“I worry that such maintenance works in the coming year will drop, especially when the construction industry’s unemployment rate is high,” he said, adding that the weak economy also played a role.

Between October and December, the industry’s unemployment rate was the highest among all sectors, at 6.7 per cent. The rate for the decoration, repair and maintenance category was even higher, at 8.3 per cent.
“Old buildings have to conduct major maintenance anyway, but residents lack the confidence. As the government is going to enhance its measures, … I hope the proposal can be announced as soon as possible,” Lam said.
Alfred Tang Yu-chi, president of the Hong Kong Institute of Construction Managers, echoed the call, saying contractors would also be affected, even though they could bid for government projects.
“The volume of public works is limited and can hardly replace the massive private maintenance market. We hope the government can set the new regime and standards as soon as possible,” he said.
Tang said contractors should be able to source the material before the Lunar New Year holiday, albeit at a higher cost.
In addition to buying from the council, he said several local suppliers also offered certified materials.
He added that some contractors reused previous mesh that had passed laboratory tests to save money.
According to Tang, a roll of mesh previously cost between HK$50 and HK$100, but now ranges from HK$120 to HK$140. Each laboratory test costs around HK$2,000, with extra charges for expedited results.
The industry estimates that scaffolding costs will rise by about 30 per cent under the new mesh inspection arrangements. Tang added that contractors might also face hidden expenses, such as fees for retesting nets damaged by storms.
He encouraged the government to establish a list of credible scaffolding net suppliers for industry reference, to help reduce repeated testing and ensure quality control.

data.title: China’s tech self-sufficiency progress evident in these 2 companies
data.description:
Chinese memory firms GigaDevice Semiconductor and Montage Technology have emerged as new heavyweights in the domestic semiconductor industry, as these companies founded by US-returned Chinese engineers capitalise on the global memory supercycle.
Since the two Shanghai-listed companies completed their dual listings in Hong Kong recently, investors have driven up the shares of GigaDevice and Montage by 151 per cent and 97 per cent, respectively.
The companies’ listings in January and February represent the rapid progress of China’s tech self-sufficiency initiative.
Notably, both companies are founded by Chinese engineers who previously worked in the US semiconductor industry.

At the centre of GigaDevice’s rise is Zhu Yiming, who also co-founded ChangXin Memory Technologies (CXMT), China’s largest DRAM (dynamic random access memory) maker. Zhu earned his master’s degree from the State University of New York at Stony Brook and spent nearly a decade in Silicon Valley before returning to Beijing in 2005 to launch GigaDevice.
GigaDevice focuses on NOR (not or) flash memory, a type of flash memory where cells are arranged horizontally in a semiconductor. Its chips provide the essential code storage that keeps devices running, from car dashboards to wearable tech.
In an area largely dominated by Taiwanese and foreign companies at the time, GigaDevice carved a niche in the mid to low-end electronics market domestically, which eventually led to an initial public offering in Shanghai in 2016.
“GigaDevice is benefiting from the triple factors of artificial intelligence development, national tech self-sufficiency and the upwards cycle of the memory industry,” said TF Securities analysts in a research note last week.
In the NOR flash market, the company currently ranks second globally with a share of 18.5 per cent, according to TF Securities.
Zhu’s true legacy, however, is his role in creating CXMT in 2016. He spearheaded China’s first serious attempt at domestic DRAM manufacturing, forming a partnership between CXMT and GigaDevice. This relationship was solidified in 2024 when GigaDevice secured a 1.9 per cent stake in CXMT.
Meanwhile, Howard Yang, Montage Technology’s co-founder, CEO and chairman, decided to focus on the “highways” of data.
With a PhD from Oregon State University, Yang at one point worked on chip design and development at US companies such as National Semiconductor, before returning to China in 1994. He co-founded Montage in 2004.
Yang zeroed in on another critical niche – memory interconnect chips, the components that manage data flow between processors and memory in massive data centres.
“We chose a future-oriented product,” Yang said in an interview with Chinese media 21st Century Business Herald in 2023. “We saw a need for a major technical breakthrough in the interface between memory and CPU [central processing units] … we spent nine years to finally get it right.”
His persistence paid off when Intel, the industry leader in CPUs, found Montage’s interconnect chip power consumption to be significantly lower than the global average, leading the US giant to cease development in that area and invest in Montage instead, Yang said.
Shanghai-based Montage was the world’s largest memory interconnect chip supplier in 2024, with 38.6 per cent of the market, according to data from Frost & Sullivan.
Now, both Montage and GigaDevice are capitalising on the global memory supercycle. The industry is in the grip of a massive supply crunch for memory chips, as a frantic ramp-up of advanced chips to feed demand from AI data centres eats up global wafer capacity, driving up costs across the supply chain.
Montage said it expected its net profit to grow between 52 per cent and 66 per cent for 2025, while Giga­Device said it expected a 46 per cent jump in net profit for last year.
With the memory supply crunch showing no signs of ebbing any time soon, analysts expect the two companies to continue to benefit.
“Having achieved sustainable scale and technical viability, these suppliers are possibly growing from domestic self-sufficiency to global exports amid a supply crunch,” said MS Hwang, a research director at Counterpoint Research.
A slew of Chinese memory firms hoping to offer Hong Kong shares “signals a strategic move in how the sector fuels its global ambitions”, said Hwang.
In January alone, at least three memory-product makers – Hosin Global Electronics, XTX Technology and Beijing XSKY Technology – submitted their Hong Kong listing applications.

data.title: Kerry Properties secures Hong Kong’s latest residential plot for US$177 million
data.description:
Hong Kong-based Kerry Properties has won a tender for a residential plot in Shau Kei Wan for HK$1.38 billion (US$177 million), which drew heavy bidding interest from both mainland Chinese and local developers.
Kerry beat seven other bidders for a 50-year grant for the 1,349-square-metre (14,523 sq ft) parcel in the Eastern district, the Lands Department said on Monday.
The plot was expected to provide a total gross floor area of over 130,000 sq ft, yielding around 300 residential units, according to Kerry.
“We are delighted that Kerry Properties has successfully secured the site at Shau Kei Wan Main Street East,” said Calvin Tong, director and general manager for Hong Kong at Kerry Properties.


The site benefits from strong transport connectivity, comprehensive lifestyle facilities and a limited supply of new residential developments in the area, he added.
The company said it planned to deliver a “high-quality project with a strong focus on sustainable development and integration with the local community, bringing new vibrancy to Shau Kei Wan and reinforcing the group’s presence in Hong Kong”.
Hong Kong developers Sun Hung Kai Properties, Kam Wah Investment, Ever Maple and K. Wah International Holdings submitted individual bids, while Sino Land and Great Eagle Holdings made a joint offer.
Mainland developers China Overseas Land and China Merchants Land submitted separate bids.
Mainland developers’ bids highlight their increased participation in Hong Kong’s land sale programme following state-owned China Overseas Land’s successful tender for a 33,712 sq ft residential plot in Kowloon East for HK$1.8 billion on February 10.
Since the start of the year, the government has held three successful rounds of land tenders. In January, a joint venture between Sino Land and Great Eagle Holdings won a 41,226 sq ft site in Jordan Valley for HK$1.61 billion.
“The higher-than-expected price for the Shau Kei Wan Main Street East site reflects developers’ positive outlook about the property market over the next few years,” said Cyrus Fong, executive director and head of valuation and advisory for Greater China at Knight Frank.
As the plot was located on Hong Kong Island, Fong said the aggressive bidding was to be expected.
Fong added that the project’s units were likely to consist of small to medium-sized flats with prices potentially reaching at least HK$25,000 per square foot.
CBRE said the size of the plot meant it was ideal for a boutique development targeting end users and investors seeking access to Hong Kong Island East.
“Its proximity to Shau Kei Wan MTR station, established schools and retail clusters supports strong absorption potential for compact to mid-sized units,” it said in an earlier statement.
“Recent improvements in overall market sentiment, coupled with strong sales performance of new residential launches, have encouraged developers to actively replenish their land banks,” the property consultancy said.
“The latest government land tenders have seen healthy participation and transaction prices exceeding initial market expectations, signalling a clear rebound in confidence.”

data.title: Large section of ceiling plunges into Hong Kong estate’s indoor pool
data.description:
The owners’ committee of a Hong Kong private housing estate has said it is “very concerned” after a large section of the ceiling collapsed into an indoor swimming pool, while the MTR Corporation, which manages the property, said a “detailed investigation” was under way to identify the cause.
In a notice issued to residents of Lake Silver in Ma On Shan, the estate’s customer service centre said it had been notified by the clubhouse contractor that “material” had fallen from the ceiling of the pool.
Images showed a large piece of debris partially submerged in the pool. A resident said the pool was closed at the time of the roof collapse.
The notice said the event had occurred on Saturday.
“Fortunately, no one was injured, and the indoor swimming pool is currently closed for a detailed inspection,” the customer service centre said.
“[The] customer service centre and the owners’ committee are very concerned about this incident.”

It added that the owners’ committee had written to the developer, Sino Land, clubhouse service contractor and the MTR Corp for “understanding the reasons and addressing these matters as soon as possible”.
In reply to queries from the South China Morning Post, the MTR Corp said that it was “highly concerned” about the incident.
“A detailed investigation is under way to identify the cause, followed by necessary follow-up and repair work,” it said.

Another image shared with the SCMP from the same estate shows a cracked ceiling in a lift lobby of one of the residential towers, with a wooden support propping it up. The SCMP has contacted the MTR Corp about the other ceiling damage.
The SCMP has approached Sino Land for comment.

data.title: Hong Kong gets ‘world party’ started with Lunar New Year night parade
data.description:
Hong Kong’s Tsim Sha Tsui district is packed with spectators, both locals and tourists, for the city’s annual Lunar New Year float parade, with festivities to mark the Year of the Horse getting into full swing.
The Cathay International Chinese New Year Night Parade began at 8pm on Tuesday with an East-meets-West street party featuring dozens of local and overseas performers, although many people arrived hours in advance to claim prime viewing spots.
This year’s event, organised by the Hong Kong Tourism Board, has the theme “Best Fortune. World Party”, with 12 floats and nearly 60 performing groups interacting with spectators along the route.


In his opening speech, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu said he was “delighted” to be celebrating the first day of the Year of the Horse at Victoria Harbour with locals and tourists.
“Over the past year, Hong Kong welcomed nearly 50 million visitors to experience our vibrant and diverse events, try our food offerings spread all across the city, and view every corner,” he said.
“Tourists’ love for Hong Kong from around the world is what keeps us wanting to improve – in the Year of the Horse, we will continue to intensify efforts to promote Hong Kong’s unique charm to global visitors.”

Rapper MC Jin got the performances rolling along with contestants from local reality television singing competitions.
Transport worker Clarence Lam, 45, arrived at Canton Road with his wife and preschooler son at 6pm to “get a good spot” after greeting relatives earlier in the day.
Lam said he hoped his son could see the performances, especially those by young children.
“Maybe some children, performers younger or older than him … will perform dragon or lion dances. I hope to broaden my son’s horizons,” he said.

His wife, Irene Tang, 40, said she wished for her son to feel the festive spirit and to see performances from all around the world.
“This is a rather large-scale festive celebration specially from Hong Kong … many Hongkongers are excited for the parade as well as the fireworks [on Wednesday],” Tang said.
Mainland Chinese tourist Lu Ming, 39, is on a two-day trip to Hong Kong with his family. The Jiangsu native, who will visit Shenzhen later on Tuesday, secured a spot next to Harbour City to catch the parade.
He said he hoped to feel the festive spirit, especially as it was the first day of Lunar New Year.
Lu, who is visiting Hong Kong for the first time, said he decided to drop by after hearing about the parade from a nearby hotel where he stayed with his family.
“[The event] is vibrant and bustling … I am very happy,” Lu said.

Tia Bjurling, 22, and Carolina Aasberg, 23, Swedish exchange students at the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, arrived at 5.45pm with a group of friends.
Bjurling said she read about the parade on Instagram. “It was a popular post that kept circulating around,” she said.
Aasberg said it was her first time in Hong Kong and that she had not celebrated Lunar New Year before. “So I was doing my research online on what to do, and this came up as one of the biggest and most interesting things to do,” she said.

Insurance broker Nathalie Bluffer, from France, visiting Hong Kong with her husband and daughter, had the much-coveted spectator seats.
“I lined up overnight physically to get the tickets,” she said. “I wanted to do it because this is the first time we are experiencing Lunar New Year in China.”
She said while the streets had been “very crowded” over the past few days, “everyone has been so nice” and the atmosphere was good.
“We’ve done celebrations before in France, but it’s not the same,” she added.

The procession departs from the Hong Kong Cultural Centre Piazza, travelling along Canton Road, Haiphong Road and Nathan Road before ending outside the Sheraton Hong Kong Hotel and Towers.
The Tourism Board earlier said that tickets for the spectator stands, released on February 7, sold out the same day, though the public could still watch for free along the route or via live-stream and television broadcasts.
Before the main parade, 15 local groups delighted crowds with warm-up performances from about 6pm, including lion dances, rope-skipping displays, street dance and magic shows.
This year’s parade features 12 themed floats blending brands, tourism icons and local culture.


Cathay Pacific Airways, which has served as the parade’s title sponsor for 24 consecutive years, will present an aviation-themed display tied to its 80th anniversary, while the Hong Kong Jockey Club’s display leans into Year-of-the-Horse imagery.
A Hong Kong Brand Toy Association float will spotlight designer-toy characters, including Labubu, while fast food chain McDonald’s joins with a nostalgic retro-style installation marking its 50-year history in Hong Kong.
Other entries recreate landmarks such as the Lam Tsuen wishing tree and feature character-filled floats from major theme parks, including Disneyland and Ocean Park.
Sixteen overseas and mainland Chinese performance troupes, including those from Australia, Italy, France, India and Egypt, are taking part, with 13 appearing in Hong Kong for the first time.

Among the highlights are Australia’s Manly Seabirds cheerleading team, a Puning Yingge dance troupe fresh from the mainland’s Spring Festival Gala, and an Italian mechanical dinosaur performance paired with Hong Kong’s luminous dragon dance.
Local acts feature prominently, too, as about 15 Hong Kong teams – from street dancers and jump-rope athletes to wheelchair ballroom dancers – will showcase home-grown talent alongside international performers.
From Wednesday – the second day of Lunar New Year – floats and performers will appear across the city. Eight floats will be displayed at Kai Tak Sports Park for the first time.
The annual parade is one of Hong Kong’s largest holiday attractions and serves as a starter for wider festive celebrations, including a fireworks display over Victoria Harbour on Wednesday, Year of the Horse race day at Sha Tin racecourse on the third day, and the Lunar New Year Cup football match on the fifth day.

data.title: Hong Kong’s first 4 babies of Year of the Horse include fifth child for a couple
data.description:
Hong Kong has welcomed at least four new babies as it enters the Year of the Horse, including a girl whose father is a talent visa holder from mainland China and a boy who is the fifth child of a local couple.
Four babies were born at midnight on Tuesday: a girl and a boy at Union Hospital in Tai Wai; a boy at Kwong Wah Hospital in Yau Ma Tei; and a girl at St Teresa’s Hospital in Kowloon City.
The boy born at Union Hospital weighed 2.94kg (6.48lbs) and was the fifth child of the Tang family.
“There’s both joy and sadness in this. When I think of a big family, it’s lively and heartwarming. I don’t feel burdened every time I think about it,” the father said.
“Be it three or four children, we raise them in the same way. Having five children also works, it’s manageable. In this social climate, being happy and living healthily are the most important. That’s already an achievement.”
The father, surnamed Zhong, of the 2.9kg girl born at Union Hospital is from Xiamen in Fujian province. Zhong came to Hong Kong two years ago via the Top Talent Pass Scheme.
“I feel that the work culture in Hong Kong and the mainland is different; our plans for her are that she will have more choices in the future,” he said.

He was with his wife for much of her labour and bought a few buns for good luck on Monday after she entered the operating room for a caesarean section.
At Kwong Wah Hospital, the new mother, surnamed Law, also gave birth via caesarean section. Despite having gestational diabetes during her pregnancy, she said it was the delivery of her 3.05kg son that was most arduous.
“The pregnancy wasn’t tough, but the C-section was. Being in labour for 24 hours was the toughest. It’s quite touching when I heard him crying from afar,” she said.

Law’s husband told reporters the baby’s due date had been last Saturday, but the couple felt lucky and surprised that their child was born in the Year of the Horse.
They were now considering using the Chinese word for horse as part of their son’s name to commemorate his birth under the zodiac sign.
At St Teresa’s Hospital, the girl born at midnight weighed 2.85kg. The baby, surnamed Ng, is the parents’ first child.

data.title: Humanoids go mainstream as China’s robotics champions appear at CCTV spectacleByteDance, Unitree, RedNote, Bilibili and Kuaishou among the firms involved in the unparalleled nationwide marketing oppor
data.description:
China’s leading humanoid robotics companies dominated the opening of this year’s Spring Festival Gala, the annual variety spectacle staged by state broadcaster CCTV that offers a rare nationwide marketing platform akin to the US Super Bowl, highlighting the central role of the industry in Beijing’s industrial policy.
Four robotics firms – Hangzhou-based Unitree, Wuxi-based Magiclab, Beijing-based Galbot and Noetix – had announced partnerships with this year’s gala in deals said to be valued at around 100 million yuan (US$14 million), after a Yangge folk dance performance by Unitree robots at last year’s event went viral and brought the industry to a mainstream audience.
The first robots to appear on Monday evening’s show were Noetix’s Bumi robots in a comedy sketch featuring a grandmother and grandson.
Unitree’s robots then performed martial arts with child artists, which included backflips and leaps from trampolines, before Magiclab’s humanoids appeared in a musical performance.


The performances showcased the progress made by humanoid technology in China thus far, with entertainment still the main outlet before they realise their longer-term potential of becoming efficient industrial workers.
Meng Siyuan, a viewer from Zhejiang province, said the choreography was “impressive”, but she was more interested in seeing human performers such as superstar Faye Wong.
“I am curious to see how robotic elements might increase year by year at the Spring Gala,” she said.
This year, ByteDance’s cloud computing arm, Volcano Engine, secured an “exclusive AI cloud partnership” with the gala, taking the baton from last year’s sponsor Alibaba Cloud, the cloud computing and artificial intelligence unit of Alibaba Group Holding. This comes as demand for computing power surges, and China’s major cloud-service providers are locked in an increasingly fierce rivalry.
ByteDance, the parent of Douyin and TikTok, is using the occasion to showcase its full-stack AI capabilities. In the run-up to the holiday, the company unveiled Seedance 2.0, a video-generation model that drew widespread attention globally, and on Saturday it released updated Doubao-Seed 2.0 series models.
In a promotion for its consumer-facing Doubao app, the company plans to distribute more than 100,000 tech products, including drones, electric vehicles, robots and 3D printers, along with digital red envelopes worth up to 8,888 yuan (US$1,286) through lucky draws on Monday.
The gesture faces off against Tencent’s Yuanbao and Alibaba’s Qwen AI apps, which before the holiday rolled out giveaways worth 1 billion yuan and 3 billion yuan, respectively, in cash and vouchers, as the ancient tradition of lucky money found a new battleground in the AI era.
Excluded by the CCTV-ByteDance partnership, Alibaba sought other avenues for Qwen, sponsoring galas organised by local broadcasters in Shanghai, Zhejiang, Jiangsu and Henan. Alibaba owns the South China Morning Post.

CCTV’s gala, though still the most-watched television programme in the country – nearly 80 per cent of active televisions were tuned to it on the eve of the Year of the Snake last year – has struggled to maintain relevance among younger viewers, who are less accustomed to sitting through live programming.
CCTV teamed up this year with RedNote, Bilibili, Kuaishou and Douyin to expand digital distribution and drive engagement, even though the show often becomes the subject of online roasting.
Other companies have also sought visibility. Dreame Technology, a Suzhou-based maker of smart home appliances, secured a commercial slot on the gala as part of an aggressive, high-profile marketing push that also included advertising during the Super Bowl in the United States.
Budget retailer Miniso and trading-card maker Kayou are among the sponsors as well, both rolling out Year of the Horse-themed merchandise tied to the festivities.

data.title: Can chatbots tell Hongkongers’ fortunes or just make them up out of thin AI-r?
data.description:
Artificial intelligence (AI) is unlikely to replace feng shui masters despite a growing trend of people turning to chatbots for fortune-telling, Hong Kong soothsayers have said, warning against blindly trusting such software as it may produce “dangerous” predictions.
Feng shui masters told the South China Morning Post that such chatbots, trained on existing datasets, could make errors, especially in metaphysics, which draws on wisdom from ancient Chinese scriptures.
Following the AI tech boom in recent years, online users have begun sharing how they consulted chatbots to read their fortunes, with a few even claiming the results were eerily accurate.
A post on the social media platform Threads that taught users how to enter prompts into chatbots received more than 1,000 likes and 2,200 shares, along with over 150 comments debating the legitimacy of AI fortune-telling.
The SCMP put two AI chatbots – Gemini and DeepSeek – to the test by asking them to predict Hong Kong’s fortune for the Year of the Horse.

Both models said 2026 would usher in the Year of the Horse, with the astrological chart overflowing with fire and lacking water, a combination they warned could lead to greater social friction. A Hong Kong feng shui master shared similar views.
“This can manifest as social restlessness, emotional outbursts or literal heat-related issues,” Gemini, which is developed by Google, said.
DeepSeek, created by a Hangzhou-based start-up, warned of a high risk of data breaches, fraud or public scandals involving established institutions, with money obtained through “aggressive, often unethical means”.
“Hong Kong’s reliance on financial services may face ‘melting’ pressure, volatility in stock exchanges or sudden shifts in global monetary policy,” it said.
But the city was likely to survive the Fire Horse better than most because of its abundant natural water in the harbour that would act as a “shock absorber”, it added.
When presented with these results, feng shui master Eric To Chung-yin agreed that fiery elements would dominate the new year, bringing volatility.
“While AI paints a picture of chaos and bad fortune, that is not entirely the case,” he explained. “Fire will bless those whose ba zi favours the element.”

Ba zi refers to the “eight characters” reflecting the cosmic time and elemental calculations of a person’s birth date.
For example, To noted that there was also strong fire energy in 2025, yet the real estate market stabilised, and the Hang Seng Index swung from a low of around 18,000 points to a high of about 27,000 points.
“It’s not completely bad all the time, just greatly fluctuating,” said To, adding that it was too simplistic to classify industries by their elements.
To said that for every 10 clients, about three or four had consulted AI to get a sense of their fortune for the year before coming to him.
Out of curiosity, he tested chatbots such as Elon Musk’s Grok, DeepSeek and the US platform Perplexity AI.
But from years of experience, To could spot that Grok made fundamental mistakes by using the wrong datasets.
He said fortune-telling was usually rooted in gan zhi, the “heavenly stems” linked to the five elements – metal, wood, water, fire and earth – as well as the energetic qualities of yin and yang.
“Perhaps Western-developed models lack ba zi data because it’s a Chinese divination method,” he said.
He agreed that DeepSeek was more accurate in gan zhi calculations but still made errors on niche combinations.
“In the short term, AI divination is still very underdeveloped. Of course, in five to 10 years, if a company invests in training the models, accuracy might improve,” he said.

Astrologer Letao Wang said he had also noticed that more clients were consulting AI for guidance before coming to him.
But he said AI, unlike fortune-tellers, could not grasp the big picture or assess someone’s personality and life direction at a glance simply from their birth chart.
“They can only identify patterns and tell you what the planetary movements mean,” he said.
Wang also warned that the platforms, developed by private businesses, might skew towards positive predictions to retain users.
“But a real astrologer will tell you things you may not want to hear,” he said.
Sunny Fung Ka-yik, a practitioner of zi wei dou shu, agreed and said he had tried platforms such as DeepAI, ChatGPT and Grok, but found no chatbot capable of analytical reasoning.
Instead of dismissing AI, Fung said he had embraced the technology in his analysis and was developing his own AI model to make accurate predictions using the methods of zi wei dou shu, a more complex form of Chinese fortune-telling grounded in astronomy.
To ensure he did not miss anything, Fung said he referred to the AI-generated analysis during his readings.
He said that when it came to calculations, AI could compute more accurately than humans, adding that he was still troubleshooting his model by identifying errors and refining its training.
But he said he did not believe AI could replace soothsayers, as such chatbots lacked a “sixth sense”.
“AI only knows how to follow formulas,” he said.
To said real-life consultations offered warmth and interactive, tailored discussions for each client’s circumstances.
“I will discuss your readings, your thoughts on them, how you should proceed and what you should avoid, but AI does not have that human empathy,” he said.
But his greatest concern was that AI chatbots often sounded extremely confident in their analysis.
“As an expert, I know how to spot the errors and correct them, or ask follow-up questions,” he said.
“But someone less knowledgeable may trust them because they sound so certain, even when the analysis is completely wrong. That is very dangerous.”

data.title: Can Hong Kong’s MPF members repeat record gains in Year of the Horse?Volatility looms in the Year of the Horse, analysts forecast, after the Year of the Snake delivered estimated earnings of US$32 bil
data.description:
The Year of the Horse is set to bring volatility for the investments of Hong Kong’s 4.8 million Mandatory Provident Fund (MPF) members, following a record-breaking Year of the Snake that ended on Monday, according to analysts.
The next zodiac year, which begins on Tuesday, is the seventh in the Chinese lunar calendar’s 12-year cycle, each represented by an animal.
The Year of the Snake delivered estimated earnings of HK$251.6 billion (US$32 billion), the highest on record since the MPF was launched in 2000, according to MPF Ratings, an independent pension research firm. Earnings and contributions pushed MPF assets to HK$1.59 trillion at the end of January.
As of February 11, the 378 MPF investment funds had gained 18.8 per cent in the Year of the Snake, much higher than the three preceding years. Members earned 12.3 per cent in the Year of the Dragon in 2024, and suffered losses of 4.5 per cent in the Year of the Rabbit in 2023 and 7.8 per cent in the Year of the Tiger in 2022, according to MPF Ratings.

Francis Chung, chairman of MPF Ratings, said the outlook for the Year of the Horse was “modest and inconclusive”, based on past records. In the last Year of the Horse in 2014, the MPF investment funds earned 2.2 per cent, while in 2002 they lost 0.4 per cent.
Chung added that Hong Kong and mainland China equity funds were expected to continue performing well. The benchmark Hang Seng Index has risen almost 4 per cent so far in 2026, after rising 28 per cent in 2025.
“This year’s Horse could well jump rather than gallop,” Chung said. “However, the risk of not riding the Horse is, in our opinion, higher than watching from the grandstand. Diversification and investing for the long term minimises uncertainty.”
The Mandatory Provident Fund Schemes Authority’s managing director Cheng Yan-chee said members should take note of market volatility due to geopolitical tensions and unclear interest-rate trends this year.
“It is important for scheme members to diversify their MPF portfolio across different asset classes and markets to better manage risks and reduce the impact of market fluctuations,” Cheng said in a statement to the South China Morning Post.
Cheng said MPF members could also consider investing in the Default Investment Strategy funds, which have a diversified investment mix and automatically shift risk levels according to members’ ages.
The best zodiac animals for the MPF were the Ox and the Rooster, according to MPF Ratings. The Year of the Ox in 2009 saw a return of 26.2 per cent, while the Year of the Rooster gained 11.3 per cent in 2005 and earned 20.4 per cent in 2017.
The worst performers were Rat and Rabbit. The Year of the Rat in 2008 saw the worst performance in MPF history, while the two previous Years of the Rabbit saw losses.
The Year of the Horse would face uncertainties, with the US Federal Reserve set to change its chairman in May and geopolitical tensions and tariffs raising the risk of market corrections, said Kenrick Chung, chief corporate solutions officer at Bay Insurance Brokers in Hong Kong.
“MPF members have to be careful in risk management,” he said. “However, the Chinese and Asian markets have a positive outlook. Hence, a positive return for MPF in 2026 is still expected.”
“Horses are always on alert – with their nearly 360-degree vision, sharp hearing and strong memory,” said Albert Leung, director at Allianz Global Investors. “Likewise, during the Year of the Fire Horse, investors should stay vigilant, watching the market closely and acting swiftly when opportunities emerge.”
MPF members could consider investing in Asia and on the mainland, where policy support was creating long-term potential in artificial intelligence, innovation and consumption, Leung said.
“Emerging-market bonds may offer additional ‘horsepower’ for investors seeking stronger income potential,” Leung said. “Overall, 2026 is shaping up to be a resilient yet complex year, where staying active, diversified and agile will be critical, much like the Fire Horse navigating shifting ground.”
Nixon Mak, head of Hong Kong pensions and solutions strategist at Invesco’s Asia-Pacific division, said the Year of the Horse would struggle to repeat the strong performance of the previous year.
“Within global equity markets, we are still favouring Asia emerging markets along with Japan, as they are the backbone of AI-data centre supplies, with fiscal support for growth in the medium term, together with a weaker US dollar,” Mak said. “European stocks could be another performer as they remain cheaper than the US from a historical perspective.”

data.title: Hong Kong crowds flock to Tai Po wishing tree for Lunar New Year blessings
data.description:
Local residents and tourists flocked to the wishing tree in Tai Po on the first day of the Lunar New Year to pray for good health, happiness and prosperity for Hong Kong.
Despite intermittent showers and temperatures of around 18 degrees Celsius (64 Fahrenheit) marking the first day of the Lunar New Year, little dampened visitors’ enthusiasm for hanging their wishing placards on the tree.
In Hong Kong, it is traditional to write one’s wishes on a card attached to an orange-shaped plastic ball and throw it onto a banyan tree in the village.
It is believed that wishes will be granted if the card is successfully hung on a branch of the tree.
“Even if it rains, I would still come,” said Wilson Chan, a 62-year-old civil servant who visits the site every Lunar New Year.
“As I get older, I wish for my good health.”
Anthony Chan, a 29-year-old finance worker, tried four or five times before successfully throwing his wishing placard onto the tree.
“I wish for everyone good health and every trade in Hong Kong beginning to flourish, as well as a lower unemployment rate,” he said.
Chan, who lives on Hong Kong Island, said he had been making his Lunar New Year wishes here for around a decade.
“The rural area has stronger festive vibes so I come here to experience it,” he said.
Another resident, retiree Lisa Li in her sixties, travelled from Fanling to wish for good health and a better environment for the city.
Tourists and foreign domestic helpers were also among the crowd making wishes.

Charlotte Friend, a 30-year-old teacher from England, travelled to Tai Po from her hotel in Wan Chai.
“I teach English in my country. Sometimes I get [students] from Hong Kong and they talk about [the tree], so I wanted to see it,” she said.
“It is very interesting because it is very different from what we have back home. We don’t have any celebrations like this.”
Friend said her new year’s wish was happiness for everyone.
Filipino domestic helper Christy Biscarra Bote, spending her first Lunar New Year in Hong Kong, travelled from Happy Valley to make a wish.
“[I wish] my employer is good to me all the time, and all my friends and family can be in good health,” she said.
Meanwhile, the Hong Kong Well-wishing Festival – a carnival with a pop-up market and booth games – will be running in the area from Tuesday until March 3.
At the festival’s opening ceremony, Acting Secretary for Constitutional and Mainland Affairs Clement Woo Kin-man extended his wishes for the country’s prosperity and the flourishing of all trades in Hong Kong.
Woo has been serving in the role since Erick Tsang Kwok-wai resigned in January, citing health reasons.
When asked for an update on the search for a minister – who will play a key role in Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu’s initiative to formulate the city’s first five-year plan – Woo did not provide any new information.
“I am acting as the [secretary] and we will coordinate on the formulation of the plan,” he said, adding that different bureaus and departments would launch consultations on the plan after China’s “two sessions” concluded in March.
Last week, the government appointed Maggie Wong Siu-chu as the Permanent Secretary for Constitutional and Mainland Affairs. She previously held an equivalent post at the Commerce and Economic Development Bureau.

data.title: How Hong Kong is galloping towards prosperity with Lunar New Year fireworks show
data.description:
Hong Kong will welcome the Year of the Horse with a 23-minute Lunar New Year fireworks display on Wednesday, showcasing vibrant elements inspired by the energy and spirit of horses.
Themed “Prosperity Gallops Across Hong Kong,” the fireworks show is solely sponsored by the Hong Kong Jockey Club at a cost of HK$19 million (US$2.4 million).
The South China Morning Post takes a closer look at the highlights of the extravaganza and the best spots to watch it from.
1. What are the highlights of the show?
At exactly 8pm, thousands of pyrotechnic shells will be launched into the sky from three barges anchored in Victoria Harbour.
According to the production company, Pyromagic Multimedia Productions, the display will stretch 850 metres (2.788 feet) across the harbour and reach up to 250 metres in height.
The show will comprise eight scenes, each with a distinct theme incorporating horse motifs and auspicious symbols.
The spectacle will open with a dazzling 15-second burst of high-density fireworks to evoke the energy of galloping horses and to bid farewell to the previous year.

Next, fireworks in the shape of the number eight and golden ingots will light up the night sky over the harbour, symbolising prosperity and abundance in the Year of the Horse.
Star-like fireworks will then appear, dancing gracefully across the sky in the fourth scene, resembling peaceful horses galloping on the horizon. This part of the show symbolises love and hope for the world and will include fireworks forming the Chinese character for good fortune.
The finale will feature numerous crown-shaped and “dragon egg” fireworks, together with a rapid cascade of high-density bursts, conveying wishes for national prosperity.
2. Where are the best vantage points?
The display will span Victoria Harbour from Central to Causeway Bay.
Wilson Mao Wai-shing of Pyromagic Multimedia said his recommendation would be the promenade outside the Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre in Wan Chai.
Other popular viewing spots include the Central waterfront next to Tamar Park, East Coast Park Precinct in North Point, the Tsim Sha Tsui promenade, and the West Kowloon Cultural District.
Another prime location is the Ocean Terminal Deck—the rooftop car park of the Harbour City shopping mall in Tsim Sha Tsui—though visitors must purchase tickets in advance, with proceeds donated to charitable organisations.

The rooftop garden of IFC Mall in Central is also a good option, while those seeking a high vantage point can head to The Peak.
Restaurants with harbour views will offer special fireworks-themed menus, often priced higher than usual.
3. What is the weather forecast on the day?
According to the Hong Kong Observatory, conditions will be relatively warm and mostly cloudy, with temperatures ranging from 17 to 22 degrees Celsius (63 to 72 Fahrenheit).
4. What public transport options will be available?
The MTR Corporation will enhance services on the East Rail Line at various times between February 13 and 23. The line connects Admiralty with the border stations at Lo Wu and Lok Ma Chau.
Service frequency on seven rail lines will be increased after the fireworks, and other road transport operators will also strengthen their connections to major checkpoints.

During peak hours, the frequency of the Hong Kong–Zhuhai–Macau Bridge shuttle bus (“Gold Bus”) will rise to about one per minute, while the Lok Ma Chau–Huanggang cross-boundary shuttle bus (“Yellow Bus”) will operate as frequently as every two minutes.
Cross-border bus services will also be enhanced.

data.title: Festive buzz in Hong Kong as visitors pour in for Lunar New Year
data.description:
Crowds filled Hong Kong’s airport and a major cross-border rail terminal on the first day of the Lunar New Year, with many visitors saying they chose to spend the holiday in the city to enjoy its festive spirit, food and mild weather.
According to the latest Immigration Department data, more than 1.7 million Hongkongers made outbound trips from February 13 to 16, up from 1.47 million in the January 25–28 period last year. They include about 320,000 residents who left the city on Monday.
Over the same period, 663,139 visitors arrived in the city, with more than 530,000 coming from mainland China.
The total number of visitors rose by 5 per cent compared with last year, while the number of mainland travellers grew by 7.1 per cent from 498,967 in 2025.
On Lunar New Year’s Eve, the city welcomed 111,757 visitors, more than 84,000 of whom were from the mainland.
The West Kowloon high-speed rail terminus was packed with travellers in bright, colourful attire on Tuesday morning, with many passengers arriving in large groups.
The children were dressed in traditional Chinese clothing, such as qipao dresses and red vests with embroidered patterns.

Ruby Luo, 36, brought five family members and their young children to Hong Kong for a two-day, one-night trip.
While the boys were rubbing their eyes after an early trip from Guangzhou, Luo, who works in the fashion industry, said she had booked a full-day visit to Disneyland for their first day in the city.
“I always come to Hong Kong with my husband, so this time I brought the whole family to spend the holiday here and show them around for the first time,” she said.
The family has planned a full itinerary for their second day, visiting attractions in Tsim Sha Tsui such as the Space Museum and K11 Musea before ending the day with the evening fireworks show.
Luo said the family planned to spend around HK$10,000 (US$1,280) a day on shopping and food and had already booked tables at a few Chinese restaurants.
Among the theme park visitors was Ji Wa, a 35-year-old pharmaceutical sales representative and mother of two. She dressed her eight-year-old daughter in festive attire for their visit to Ocean Park on the first day of their three-day trip.
She was keen to try a local cha chaan teng restaurant and stroll around the city before returning to Shantou.
Same-day traveller Huan Du, 33, had no specific plans and simply came to walk around Central and Victoria Harbour with her daughter and two friends.
“I saw videos on Douyin about good places to eat in Hong Kong, so I just wanted to check them out,” said the professional in the construction industry, referring to the popular Chinese short video platform.

At the airport, Martin Wang from Zhejiang province on the mainland said he often visited Hong Kong for work, but this was his first time spending Lunar New Year in the city with his wife and their children, aged seven and 11.
“It is much warmer here,” said the manufacturing business owner in his forties, when asked why he chose to spend Lunar New Year in Hong Kong. “I also wanted to bring my children to experience a cosmopolitan city.”
The family planned to visit Golden Bauhinia Square in Wan Chai and Victoria Harbour for the Lunar New Year parade, with Wang saying he expected to spend about HK$20,000 in the city on accommodation and shopping for the four of them.
Jeff Yin, a 10-year-old student from Shanghai, arrived in Hong Kong with his parents and said it was his first time in the city. The family of three planned to visit Disneyland and the University of Hong Kong.
“We will be spending six days here,” said Yin’s father, who did not give his name. “We may go shopping, too, but we haven’t set a budget yet.”
Many Hongkongers also chose to spend the festival outside the city.
Resident Iris Lee, a mother of two in her mid-30s, took the train to Dongguan with her husband and in-laws to visit relatives on the mainland for two days, part of their annual Lunar New Year tradition.
“We go back every year to visit [my husband’s] family and gather together,” she said. “It isn’t a hassle to bring the children; many relatives there help look after them.”
The clerk’s five-year-old daughter and two-year-old son had packed a colourful suitcase and were excited to deliver festive greetings in Chinese.
Administrative clerk Samantha Wong travelled to Malaysia to celebrate Lunar New Year with her 24-year-old daughter, a student in Kuala Lumpur, and plans to stay until March 5.
“My trip is also to help sort out some things for my daughter, so I haven’t planned much sightseeing. But if we have any spare time, we might check out the Twin Towers,” the mother, who is in her fifties, said.

data.title: Can Hong Kong lead a digital gold trading revolution with blockchain innovation?
data.description:
This Lunar New Year, Hongkongers can gift friends and family digital gold tokens, a new way of blending the city’s tradition of giving gold for good wishes with the convenience of blockchain technology.
HSBC added this new transfer feature earlier this month to its retail gold token, which represents fractional ownership of physical gold stored in a secure vault and is the only retail-focused gold token approved by Hong Kong’s Securities and Futures Commission.
The bank’s enhancement to its gold token, which has racked up over US$1 billion in trading volume in the two years since launch, highlights a rising appetite for tokenised real-world assets as Hong Kong doubles down on digital finance.
Banks are not the only institutions rolling out tokenised gold. Asset managers, fintech firms and cryptocurrency companies are coming up with innovative products for investors to gain exposure to gold with the added transparency of blockchain.
Tokenised gold was giving investors a new way to hold the metal, easing pain points around access, custody, transfer and trading, according to experts. The growth comes amid record gold prices, a hunt for diversification and the rise of stablecoins and digital money, which could make it easier to move in and out of digital assets.

Hong Kong aims to approve its first batch of stablecoin issuers by March, as part of a wider regulatory drive to develop its digital-asset market. The city has also stepped up efforts to build a broader gold ecosystem, from trading and clearing to storage.
“I see demand for tokenised gold increasing while global political uncertainty remains,” said Mike Foy, chief financial officer of Amina Bank. The Swiss lender’s five-year-old gold token saw record subscriptions in the last six months of last year.
He expected tokenised gold to be a key part of investors’ portfolios in the mid to long term as “it often costs less than traditional ETFs [exchange-traded funds], has greater transparency due to the blockchain and the transaction friction is significantly less”.
“Tokenised gold is unlikely to replace physical bullion, but it can become a dominant digital interface for trading and portfolio management, much like [how] ETFs became the main vehicle for price exposure without displacing physical gold,” said Daniel Rabetti, an assistant professor for accounting and finance at the National University of Singapore Business School.
In 2025, daily turnover in PAXG, one of the largest bullion-backed tokens issued by New York-based Paxos, was several times higher than GLD, the world’s biggest gold ETF run by State Street Global Advisors, even though PAXG was backed by only about US$2 billion of gold, compared with more than US$170 billion in GLD holdings as of early February.
“People have used gold for 8,000 years, and it always costs you something to own gold – for storage, for transport,” said Iggy Ioppe, chief investment officer at Theo, a crypto trading infrastructure start-up. “Putting things on chain and tokenising real-world assets unlocks a whole world of decentralised finance [DeFi].”
Theo marks a more leveraged push to turn tokenised gold into a yield-bearing product designed for DeFi venues.
Last month, the firm launched tokenised gold built on FundBridge Capital’s on-chain gold fund and supported by Standard Chartered’s tokenisation platform, Libeara. The fund lends money to gold retailers against their inventory, so investors still track the gold price but earn interest on these loans, subject to counterparty, collateral and market risks.
Ioppe said he saw DeFi as having a “Lego blocks” structure as it was permissionless, required no minimum investment or unnecessary paperwork and was truly global, making it easy to stack and build new financial services.
Tokenised gold in DeFi could be put to work rather than being just held, using it as collateral or combining it with stablecoins in yield‑generating strategies, according to Rabetti.
However, many existing regulations on stablecoins focus on physical backing, anti-money-laundering and investor protection, limiting applications in the DeFi space.
How tokenised gold would take shape in DeFi remained unclear, as success would hinge on transparent proof of reserves, said Amina Bank’s Foy.
“The key distinction is not ‘DeFi versus traditional finance’, but whether tokenisation allows gold to be used more flexibly across markets and platforms, while still anchored to trusted custody and legal structures,” said David Chan, managing director and partner at Boston Consulting Group.
This is where Hong Kong had the edge, with progress in overall tokenisation enabled by three forces: regulatory clarity, government support and market innovation, he added.
“Hong Kong has a sophisticated gold investor base and a strong physical and financial gold ecosystem,” Chan said. “Adoption [of tokenised gold] will ultimately depend on tangible investor benefits rather than the novelty of structure.”

data.title: Chow Tai Fook-linked firms set for record US$192 million hit in Giordano case
data.description:
In the largest settlement for a Takeovers Code breach in Hong Kong, the Securities and Futures Commission (SFC) has reached an agreement with two wholly owned subsidiaries of Chow Tai Fook Nominee (CTFN) – a company linked to the Henry Cheng Ka-shun family – requiring them to pay up to HK$1.5 billion (US$192 million) to independent shareholders of Giordano International.
The agreement with Sino Wealth International, Giordano’s majority shareholder, and Clear Prosper Global followed an investigation into share dealings dating back to 2016, which the regulator said involved breaches of the code by CTFN and parties acting in concert with it.
After examining Giordano’s shareholding structure, the SFC concluded that two additional shareholders – Best Sincere and Firstrate Enterprises, both outside the CTFN group – had been acting in concert with the group to acquire or consolidate control of the company.
Their combined shareholdings and dealings should therefore have been counted under the Takeovers Code, the regulator said in a statement on Monday.
Best Sincere first acquired Giordano shares on April 26, 2016. The investment holding company, incorporated in May 2005, was previously owned by a former director of companies within the CTFN group and is now held by his son.
Firstrate first bought Giordano shares about 18 years ago. Incorporated in October 2007, it has been held by another director within the CTFN group, according to the regulator.

The concert group’s stake in Giordano crossed the 30 per cent threshold on May 18, 2016, triggering an obligation to make a general offer that same day.
“However, no general offer was made for Giordano as required,” the SFC said.
Separately, Clear Prosper announced a voluntary general offer on September 13, 2022, but the offer lapsed even though its only condition had been met.
“Under the settlement, Sino Wealth and Clear Prosper have agreed to make cash payments to independent shareholders who held Giordano shares on the dates of the breaches,” the SFC statement said.
“Subject to the number of successful claims received, the maximum payment payable could potentially involve up to approximately HK$1.5 billion,” it said.
Sino Wealth and Clear Prosper have agreed to implement the payment, but they denied and continue to deny all assertions, according to an announcement by Giordano on Monday.
Giordano’s shares rose 0.67 per cent to HK$1.51 on Monday, the final trading day of the Year of the Snake.

data.title: Hong Kong airport’s second terminal to fully open, serve 15 airlines from May 27
data.description:
The Hong Kong airport’s second terminal will fully open on May 27, authorities have announced, with the redeveloped facility set to serve as the base for 15 airlines.
The terminal, which will mostly handle regional flights, is part of the airport’s HK$141.5 billion (US$18.1 billion) expansion project launched in 2016 that also included a third runway. Its coach hall opened in September.
Fred Lam Tin-fuk, chairman of the Airport Authority, said on Tuesday that the first terminal, used mainly for long-haul flights, had been very busy.
“We hope the second terminal can share some of the flight load,” he said.

The airlines expected to operate out of the second terminal include Hong Kong Airlines, HK Express and Greater Bay Airlines.
Lam emphasised the seamless connection between the two terminals, both accessible via the Airport Express. Train doors open on the left to the first terminal and on the right to the new second terminal.
Secretary for Transport and Logistics Mable Chan said the two terminals would complement each other and “greatly enhance” the airport’s capacity.
She hoped the redevelopment would boost passenger numbers beyond last year’s 61 million trips.
The expansion project, particularly the new three-runway system, has been hailed as a “game-changer” for strengthening the city’s status as an international aviation hub.

The South China Morning Post previously reported that about 20 per cent of all departing passengers were expected to complete their check-in and baggage drop-off at the second terminal.
It will feature more self-service facilities, including smart check-in kiosks and self-service bag-drop counters, according to the authority.
Developer New World Development’s retail and entertainment complex, 11 Skies, was a key component of the second terminal, the authority said earlier, describing the centre as “a theme park offering experiences for children, families and visitors”.

data.title: Beware excessive ‘fire energy’ in Year of the Horse: Hong Kong feng shui masters
data.description:
Hongkongers and world leaders should take a step back and be careful to avoid conflicts during the Year of the Horse, feng shui masters have warned, saying the zodiac sign contains excessive “fire energy” that could trigger aggressive action.
Soothsayers interviewed by the South China Morning Post said the coming year, which fell under the category of the “fire horse” in the lunar calendar, would remain volatile for China and crisis-prone for the US. They also predicted that Hong Kong would remain embroiled in geopolitical tensions, even as its economy improved gradually.
The horse is one of the 12 zodiac signs in Chinese cosmology, while fire is one of its five elements – metal, wood, water, fire and earth – whose characteristics and powers affect those born that year and beyond.
Celebrity host and feng shui master Qixianyu, better known as Master Seven, urged Hongkongers to avoid conflict with others and be cautious, as fiery tempers, clashes and weapons were all governed by the fire energy.
“If you engage in conflict with others, a small stressful situation could result in violence,” she said.
Astrologer Letao Wang said that while the energy of the Year of the Snake was more introspective, the image of the fire horse was forward-moving.
Wang gave similar advice to world leaders: “Think and rethink before you take action, specifically anything related to war, military, impatience and aggression.
“The snake represents knowledge and wisdom, so what wisdom did you gain last year? You would have to ride the horse based on that – if that self-reflection is not done well, the horse will run wild.”
Geomancer Yunwenzi, or Master Cloud, said Hong Kong’s fortune for the coming year would be “relatively weak” and the geopolitical outlook would “remain somewhat clouded”, according to Qimen Dunjia, another form of divination from ancient China.
“Balancing the relationship between the central government and Hong Kong, as well as managing cooperation models with the international community, will remain critical tasks for Hong Kong,” she said.
She said Hong Kong’s economy would improve slowly, with stocks seeing an overall positive trend despite market sentiment being highly unstable throughout the year, driven by volatility from the United States.
“Investors should exercise extra caution, particularly in April, June, July and August, as these months will see large fluctuations and significant risks,” she said.
In 2026, she said the most flourishing sectors would be finance and trade, along with high-end professional services.

Fortune-teller Bowie Tsang named more professions that would benefit in the Year of the Fire Horse.
“Industries associated with ‘fire’ or ‘mobility’ tend to perform better, such as pharmaceuticals, healthcare, beauty, aesthetic medicine, tourism, entertainment and culture,” she said.
According to Tsang, the spring and summer in the Year of the Horse could be beneficial for the economy and the stock market, with the two seasons symbolising wood and flames and complementing the year’s intense fire energy.
She added that the metal and water elements would strengthen in the autumn and winter, restraining the fire energy and leading to more conservative sentiments and greater pressure in the stock and speculative markets.
As for Hong Kong, due to the strong performance in the finance sector in the first half of the year, the city’s role and function on the global stage would remain stable while going through a “period of recalibration”, Tsang said.
Her reminder for Hong Kong businesses and individuals was that it was of paramount importance to maintain flexibility and their unique selling points while avoiding rigid adherence to outdated models or singular positioning.
Master Seven predicted a good year for Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu, saying his ba zi – the “eight characters” that reflect cosmic time and elemental calculations of a person’s birth date – contains many water elements.
The fortune-teller said a person imbued with heavy water elements would be favoured in a year with strong fire energy, meaning that the current fire period would boost his fortunes.
If Lee, who took the helm in 2022, could secure a second five-year term in government, Wang said his career would flourish starting from the second half of 2027, leading into 2028, and also bring the city prosperity.
Wang said in Western astrology, 2026 would also witness the conjunction of Neptune and Saturn in the fire sign Aries, a planetary event that last occurred over 1,400 years ago.
Aries represents leadership and aggression, while Neptune and Saturn rule idealism and practicality, respectively, he said.
“It means this is the time for not just the individual, but for our world leaders, to find the middle point between the vision versus practicality,” he said.

For China, Master Cloud said the Year of the Fire Horse would bring significant volatility to the nation’s fortunes as companies would inevitably remain mired in price wars.
Political pressure on China this year would be extreme, potentially leading to the adoption of grey zone tactics, shifting towards economic infiltration or localised blockades rather than direct military action, she said.
For US President Donald Trump, who was born in the Year of the Dog, celebrity fortune-teller Mak Ling-ling said he would see good fortune and new opportunities, as the dog enjoyed a friendly relationship with the horse.
But Master Cloud said the fortune of the United States in 2026 could fall into an ominous pattern, signalling a year of disaster, crisis, and intense struggle or “warfare”.
“The US will face multidimensional pressure, including economic blowback, a move by allies to reduce their reliance on the US, and the intensification of internal conflicts,” she said.

data.title: Global AI race heats up as Chinese tech giant releases new model
data.description:
Alibaba Cloud has unveiled its next-generation open artificial intelligence model, Qwen-3.5, in a move set to have major implications for the global AI race between China and the US.
The much-anticipated release, timed on the eve of the Lunar New Year, capped a frenetic week in which nearly every major Chinese AI developer rolled out new flagship models.
The two new 3.5-series models were made available on Alibaba’s cloud platform Model Studio on Monday afternoon.
The new Qwen-3.5-Open-Source model with 397 billion parameters showed significant improvement over the company’s previous flagship model, Qwen-3-Max-Thinking, despite the latter’s much larger size of over 1 trillion parameters, according to self-reported benchmark scores.
The model also boasted a performance on par with leading models from OpenAI, Anthropic and Google DeepMind, the benchmarks showed, although the comparison was not with the three US heavyweights’ latest models.
Meanwhile, a closed-source version called Qwen-3.5-Plus achieved performance “on par with state-of-the-art leading models” and has a listed context window – the amount of data it can process at any given time – of 1 million tokens, one of the largest in the industry.
Parameters are the mathematical variables that shape how an AI system learns and reasons. Alibaba Cloud is the AI and cloud computing arm of Alibaba Group Holding, owner of the South China Morning Post.
For the first time, Qwen-3.5 is built with native multimodal capabilities, allowing the models to understand text, images, audio and video within a single system.
The model family also adopts the company’s latest architecture, first previewed in September through an experimental system called Qwen3-Next, designed to improve computational efficiency and reduce operating costs.
This helped the model to achieve “a new benchmark for capability per unit of inference cost”, the company said.
Model weights – the parameters encoding the systems’ capabilities or “intelligence” – were released on developer platforms Hugging Face and Alibaba’s own ModelScope, enabling users with sufficient hardware to download and run the models locally.
Over the past year, China has positioned itself as a leading force in open-source AI, distinguishing its approach from the more closed-model strategies favoured by many Silicon Valley giants that do not publicly release model weights.

Download data from Hugging Face showed Chinese open models overtook US counterparts last year in global adoption, with DeepSeek and Qwen accounting for the bulk of that growth.
Qwen, in particular, has gained traction for its breadth of use cases, as Alibaba typically releases multiple versions of the same base models at different sizes to drive uptake across developers and enterprises, as well as its multilingual support.
The new Qwen-3.5 models added 82 new languages and dialects from the previous Qwen generation to support 201 languages and dialects, including niche languages such as Hawaiian and Fijian, according to the latest announcement.
Such features have helped establish Qwen as a de facto “open model standard”, according to open-model expert Nathan Lambert, who noted that downloads of Qwen on Hugging Face in December exceeded those of all other major open models combined.
China’s growing prominence in the open-model ecosystem prompted the Trump administration last year to prioritise the global diffusion of US-developed open models.
However, a more telling metric would be real-world deployment rather than downloads alone, said Lennart Heim, an AI and semiconductor analyst tracking US-China competition.
“Model downloads don’t say anything about actual usage,” he said, adding that the national-security implications of open Chinese models remained uncertain, even as some US start-ups and academic institutions deployed them locally.
Notably, Alibaba does not open-source all of its systems. Its largest models by parameter count, known as the Max series, remain closed and closely integrated with the company’s flagship consumer app, Qwen.
At the same time, the company has pursued an aggressive commercialisation strategy, positioning open models at the centre of its cloud business by bundling data hosting and model-inference services for developers and enterprises worldwide.
On X, technical lead of Alibaba Cloud’s Qwen team Lin Junyang suggested that they would release more open-weight models in the next few days.

data.title: Thousands flock to Hong Kong temple for Lunar New Year incense stick ritual
data.description:
Thousands of worshippers flocked to Hong Kong’s best-known Taoist temple on Monday for the annual ritual of the burning of the first incense sticks, marking the start of Lunar New Year with prayers for prosperity and health in the Year of the Horse.
The Sik Sik Yuen Wong Tai Sin Temple threw open its doors at 9pm on Monday for the traditional Lunar New Year’s Eve celebration, welcoming crowds eager to offer their first sticks of incense to the Great Immortal Wong at 11pm.
The Kowloon landmark will remain open overnight and throughout the first day of Lunar New Year to accommodate the thousands expected to pray for good fortune, closing its gates at 9pm on Tuesday.
Veteran actress Lana Wong Ha-wai, 95, known for her extravagant annual ritual outfits, appeared this year dressed as Guanyin, the Goddess of Mercy.

She said she was seeking “double protection” from the deities following a gruelling 12-year legal battle to reclaim an inheritance “stolen” from her.
“Now that the funds have been recovered, I’m working with my lawyers to finalise the transfer. I will use the money immediately to help build a nursing home for the elderly,” Wong said.
“I also wish for Hongkongers to enjoy good health, world peace and the security of a stable home.”
Renowned for his supposed healing powers, the Taoist deity Great Immortal Wong, widely known as Wong Tai Sin, remains a focal point of local devotion.
According to folk tradition, those who manage to plant the very first incense sticks at the temple’s main altar are believed to be bestowed with the greatest abundance of blessings for the coming year.
Crowd control measures were strictly enforced at the temple, with worshippers required to follow a one-way flow of movement.
Under these restrictions, the fortune-telling and stalls for interpreting oracle sticks were temporarily closed to the public, and devotees were limited to bringing only nine sticks of incense into the temple grounds.
Despite these measures, many attendees arrived in high spirits and elaborate costumes – some sporting Year of the Horse headpieces. Dedicated worshippers began gathering as early as noon, waiting hours for the chance to offer the “first incense” at midnight to secure Wong Tai Sin’s blessings for the coming year.

Among those waiting was app programmer Paul Cheung Lok-man, 42, who has made the pilgrimage a personal tradition.
“After the [Covid-19] pandemic in 2023, I came here to offer the first incense for the first time and felt my luck truly improved. It has since become our Lunar New Year tradition. This year, I’m praying for a better job opportunity,” Cheung said.
University student Alice Yeung Yuen-yan, 21, was attending to help her 75-year-old grandmother.
“Since my grandmother has mobility issues, I came along to accompany her. We even prepared folding stools for the long wait. Her prayers are simple – she just wants us all to be healthy,” she said.
“As for me, I hope to find a dream job after graduation and travel more to see the world.”
Sik Sik Yuen, the charity that runs the temple, earlier said it expected between 20,000 and 40,000 worshippers to pack the grounds on Monday night for the first incense sticks burning ritual as Hong Kong ushered in Lunar New Year.
The temple is bracing for a massive turnout for the festive period, with total visitor numbers estimated to reach 1 million throughout the holidays.
